skill.name: github-elements-tracking
skill.description: Coordinates long-running development work through GitHub Issues, GitHub CLI workflows, git worktrees, checkpoints, and SERENA memory-bank handoffs.
skill.summary: Track multi-session AI development work with GitHub Issues, checkpoints, phase handoffs, and recovery playbooks.
skill.author: Emasoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, environment, and network patterns, but contextual review shows this package is Markdown workflow documentation rather than executable hidden code. The main confirmed risk is that the playbooks instruct agents to run GitHub CLI, git, shell, workflow, and external tool setup commands, so publication is acceptable with an operational safety warning.

medium_findings.title: Operational Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to run GitHub CLI, git, shell, and external setup commands. This is expected for a GitHub workflow skill, but commands can modify repositories, install MCP tooling, create labels, create workflows, change branches, and affect issue or PR state if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are direct and numerous, and their operational impact is clear. They are part of the skill purpose rather than hidden malicious code, so the risk is medium instead of high.

medium_findings.title: Repository and Filesystem Modification Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill tells background agents where they may write, creates worktrees, removes worktrees, and writes report files. These instructions are useful for coordination but can alter local repository state and should be run in the intended project only.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines explicitly permit writes and show worktree cleanup commands. The scope is documented and not malicious, but the commands can affect local files.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Names in Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged database or environment access, but the reviewed content shows example configuration names such as REDIS_URL and documented safeguard variables. No real secret value or credential exfiltration instruction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain documentation of variable names and example internal URLs. It should still remind users not to post real secrets in issues.

low_findings.title: Benign Relative Paths and Public Avatar URL
low_findings.description: Path traversal and network findings are mostly relative asset paths and one public GitHub avatar URL in an issue comment template. These are not file-read or exfiltration logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are a Markdown table of avatar paths and a public GitHub image URL. There is no executable code that reads arbitrary paths.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Windows SAM Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Several high and critical scanner hits are keyword collisions in prose, such as JWT examples, RS256 review text, and the word SAME. No evidence of weak cryptographic implementation or Windows SAM database access was found in the reviewed Markdown context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show coordination prose and review checklist examples, not code that implements cryptography or accesses operating system credential stores.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Shell and GitHub CLI Blocks
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains copy-paste command blocks that can change labels, branches, worktrees, GitHub Actions files, issues, and pull requests. Users should inspect commands and run them with least-privilege credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is confirmed by concrete command blocks. It is dangerous if blindly executed, but it matches the stated workflow-management purpose.

content.value_statement: Long-running AI development work can lose context across sessions and agents. This skill provides GitHub Issue protocols, checkpoints, worktree guidance, and recovery steps for durable coordination.
actual_capabilities: Defines phase-based GitHub Issue workflows for DEV, TEST, REVIEW, and epic coordination.
Provides checkpoint formats for preserving work state across context exhaustion and session handoffs.
Documents git worktree patterns for parallel agents working on separate branches.
Includes GitHub CLI examples for creating issues, posting comments, labels, and phase transitions.
Describes SERENA MCP memory-bank integration for project-level context and large documents.
Supplies templates for issue comments, status reports, recovery notes, and validation checklists.
limitations: The skill is documentation and workflow guidance; it does not enforce policies by itself.
Commands assume GitHub CLI authentication and repository permissions are already configured.
SERENA MCP steps depend on external tools that may not be installed in every environment.
Users must review generated GitHub Actions and shell commands before applying them to production repositories.

use_cases.title: Recover Work After Context Loss
use_cases.description: Use issue checkpoints and recovery walkthroughs to resume partially completed work after compaction or session interruption.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Parallel Feature Work
use_cases.description: Use epics, wave labels, scope declarations, and worktrees to reduce conflicts between agents working on related tasks.

use_cases.title: Create Auditable Review Gates
use_cases.description: Use DEV, TEST, and REVIEW phase protocols to document test runs, verdicts, bug triage, and final approval history.

prompt_templates.title: Start Tracking a Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use github-elements-tracking to create a GitHub Issue workflow for this task. Include labels, scope declaration, checkpoint format, and first next action.

prompt_templates.title: Recover a Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the recovery protocol to inspect the existing issue thread and reconstruct completed work, pending work, blockers, branch, and next action.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Agent Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Coordinate an epic with parallel tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the epic coordinator playbook to divide this project into waves, assign DEV, TEST, and REVIEW phases, and define conflict checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Workflow Enforcement
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the proposed GitHub Actions enforcement setup for this repository. Identify unsafe assumptions, permission needs, and rollout steps before installation.

output_examples.input: Track this authentication feature across sessions.
output_examples.output: A GitHub Issue plan with DEV, TEST, and REVIEW phase labels, a scope declaration, checkpoint sections, branch naming, and recovery instructions.

output_examples.input: I lost context during a test phase. Recover the state.
output_examples.output: A recovery summary listing the last checkpoint, completed tests, failing tests, changed files, current branch, blockers, and the next safe action.

output_examples.input: Set up an epic for three agents working in parallel.
output_examples.output: An epic workflow with wave ordering, issue ownership rules, scope conflict checks, worktree guidance, and review gates.

best_practices: Review every shell, GitHub CLI, and workflow command before running it in a repository.
Post checkpoints after meaningful state changes so recovery does not depend on local chat history.
Use least-privilege GitHub tokens and test enforcement workflows in a non-production repository first.
anti_patterns: Do not run copied workflow or shell snippets without checking repository permissions and branch state.
Do not store secrets, private keys, or production credentials in GitHub Issues or checkpoint comments.
Do not let multiple agents modify the same files without a posted scope declaration and conflict check.

faq.question: Does this skill run code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown playbooks and command examples. The user or agent chooses whether to run commands.

faq.question: Why does the audit flag command execution?
faq.answer: The documentation includes many GitHub CLI, git, shell, and workflow examples. These are expected but require review before use.

faq.question: Can this skill recover work after context exhaustion?
faq.answer: Yes. It defines checkpoint and recovery formats that let a new session rebuild state from GitHub Issues.

faq.question: Does it require SERENA MCP?
faq.answer: The workflow recommends SERENA MCP for large documents and project memory. Basic GitHub Issue tracking can still be reviewed separately.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used in private repositories, but users should avoid posting secrets or sensitive business data into issue comments.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is best for teams or operators coordinating AI agents across long-running development, testing, review, and release workflows.
